coredata.dc:title: Carbon dots from green sources as efficient sensor and as anticancer agent
coredata.prism:publicationName: Journal of Photochemistry and Photobiology A: Chemistry
coredata.dc:creator: Mathew, Sneha
Thara, Chinnu R.
John, Neenamol
Mathew, Beena
coredata.dc:description: This work reports the synthesis of novel fluorescent carbon dots (CD) from green sources via the hydrothermal method for detecting the antibiotic doxycycline. The blue fluorescent carbon dots with an average diameter of 2.64 nm exhibited outstanding stability towards different experimental conditions. The sensing of doxycycline by CDs is due to its fluorescent quenching through the inner filter effect (IFE), which served as the basis for its quantification. In addition, these carbon dots were employed for practical applications like the detection of doxycycline residues in real water samples, and tablets. Moreover, a glassy carbon electrode (GCE) modified with CDs exhibited as a novel electrochemical sensor for doxycycline with a detection limit of 2.86 nM. Also, the anticancer activity of CDs against MCF-7 breast cancer cells is demonstrated. The developed carbon dots have remarkable potential as a dual sensor for the antibiotic doxycycline and anticancer applications.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Simarouba glauca
Fluorescence
Doxycycline
Electrochemical analysis
Anticancer agent

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Detection of antibiotics in real samples
Detection of antibiotics by the CDs
Reproducibility, stability analysis, PEIS, and photocurrent density
Possible mechanism for the electrochemical sensing of doxycycline
Detection of doxycycline in real samples

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: Greatly acknowledges the financial assistance received from the University Grants Commission (UGC) in the form of Junior Research Fellowship (JRF).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) TEM image of CDs, inset shows HR-TEM image of CDs, (b) SAED pattern of CDs, (c) particle size distribution histogram of CDs.
(a) FT-IR, and (b) Raman spectra of carbon dots.
(a) XPS spectra of CDs survey scan and high-resolution spectra of (b) C1s, (c) O1s, and (d) N1s.
The optical properties of the CDs. (a) UV–vis. absorption spectra with maximum emission and excitation, (b) PL emission spectra of carbon dot at different excitation wavelengths.
Fluorescence spectra showing (a) ionic stability in Nacl, (b) KCl, (c) photostability, and (d) pH stability of CD.
(a) The selectivity study, (b) fluorescence response of CD in presence of doxycycline, (c) relationship between F0/F and concentration of doxycycline, and (d) interference study.
The mechanism of doxycycline detection by CDs (a) Maximum excitation-emission spectra and UV– vis spectra of doxycycline, (b) UV spectra of CD, Dox, and CD + Dox, and (c) lifetime measurements.
(a) CV runs of bare GCE, CD/GCE, and CD/GCE/Dox, (b) effect of pH, (c) effect of scan rate, and (d) square root of scan rate vs current.
(a) Selectivity study, (b) DPV of CD with different concentrations of doxycycline, and (c) plot of concentration of doxycycline vs peak current.
CV runs showing (a) stability, (b) repeatability and reusability, (c) impedance spectra, and (d) photocurrent response.
Detection of doxycycline in real samples.
Microscopic images of MCF-7 cells after incubation of 24 h with various concentrations of CDs (a) 0, (b) 6.5, (c) 12.5, (d) 25, (e) 50, and (f) 100 µL/mL using MTT assay.
Cell viability of carbon dots on MCF-7 (Human breast cancer) cells.
The schematic of carbon dot synthesized by Simaroubaglauca leaves and detection of doxycycline.
Comparison of various methods for doxycycline detection.

ce:legend.ce:simple-para: SWV- square wave voltammetry, LSV- linear sweep voltammetry.

head.ce:title: Carbon dots from green sources as efficient sensor and as anticancer agent

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Chemical Sciences, Mahatma Gandhi University, Kottayam 686560, Kerala, India
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Chemical Sciences, Mahatma Gandhi University, Kottayam 686560, Kerala, India

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: The schematic of carbon dot synthesized by Simarouba glauca leaves and detection of doxycycline

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: This work reports the synthesis of novel fluorescent carbon dots (CD) from green sources via the hydrothermal method for detecting the antibiotic doxycycline. The blue fluorescent carbon dots with an average diameter of 2.64 nm exhibited outstanding stability towards different experimental conditions. The sensing of doxycycline by CDs is due to its fluorescent quenching through the inner filter effect (IFE), which served as the basis for its quantification. In addition, these carbon dots were employed for practical applications like the detection of doxycycline residues in real water samples, and tablets. Moreover, a glassy carbon electrode (GCE) modified with CDs exhibited as a novel electrochemical sensor for doxycycline with a detection limit of 2.86 nM. Also, the anticancer activity of CDs against MCF-7 breast cancer cells is demonstrated. The developed carbon dots have remarkable potential as a dual sensor for the antibiotic doxycycline and anticancer applications.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: No data was used for the research described in the article.

ce:section.ce:para: Doxycycline (Dox) is a tetracycline-class antibiotic with β-diketonate structure that can be used to treat sensitive bacterial infections in the respiratory, urinary, and biliary systems. In animal breeding, it is commonly utilized in veterinary medicine and provides a broad range of antibacterial properties against Gram-positive and Gram-negative bacteria [1,2]. Dox is widely utilized in the treatment of animal husbandry and as feed, additives to stimulate animal growth due to its broad-spectrum activity and cost-effectiveness [3]. However, doxycycline residue in water bodies and ordinary foods can cause gastrointestinal problems, hepatotoxicity, mentation abnormalities in children, yellow teeth, and; resistance to antibiotics. Also, misuse of Dox would result in widespread antibiotic residues in foods like honey, meat, and milk, posing a major health risk to humans [4]. The European Union (EU) has set maximum residual limits (MRLs) for doxycycline in mussels, 0.3 mg/ kg in skin and fat, 0.6 mg/kg in the kidney and, 0.3 mg/kg in the liver to guarantee that food is fit for human consumption [5]. As a result, scientifically and technically, developing a novel strategy to serve antibiotics with sensitivity and selectivity is a key challenge.

To identify tetracycline antibiotics, a variety of test methods have been developed, including electrochemical sensors [6], UV– vis. absorbance spectroscopy (UV–vis) [7], high-performance liquid chromatography [8], flow-injection analysis [9], and fluorescence spectrophotometry. Among these, fluorescence spectrophotometry is the fast and cost-effective method [10]. To detect Dox, a variety of fluorescence sensors have been developed, including CDs [11] and metal–organic coordination polymers [12]. Interestingly, carbon dot finds extensive application as fluorescent and electrochemical sensors.

The appearance of fluorescent carbon dots has recently had a significant impact on the advancement of various fields such as catalysis, electronics, medicine, energy, and photonics and has piqued interest due to their ease of preparation, low toxicity, photostability, and biocompatibility [13]. These properties make CDs to open the door for a variety of applications, including detection [14,15], bioimaging [16], photocatalysis [17,18], ink [19] etc. Even at very low concentrations, inorganic pollutants like heavy metals causes severe pollution and results in a variety of detrimental consequences on human health. CDs have become one of the most promising materials for the detection of heavy metal ions because of their exceptional fluorescence capabilities [20–22]. Additionally, CDs can be used to identify organic pollutants like antibiotics [23,24], pesticides [25,26], and dyes [27] due to their distinct chemical structure and abundant group.

Carbon dots were first synthesized using carbonaceous materials after their discovery. However, the popularity of green synthetic techniques has prompted a surge an interest in green carbon dot synthesis. Green carbon dots are carbon dots made with ‘green precursors' as a carbon source. The phrase ‘green precursors’ refers to naturally occurring chemicals or derivatives of renewable natural products or processes [28]. Fluorescence (FL) and electrochemical-based sensing have received a lot of interest in recent years because of their advantages, such as high sensitivity, quick response time, and low cost [29,30]. Based on the aforementioned considerations, we have attempted to build a multi-mode CD sensing platform for doxycycline.

Here we report the synthesis of carbon dots from Simarouba glauca leaves. Simarouba glauca (S.glauca, abbreviated as SG), widely known as ‘Laxmitaru’ or ‘Paradise tree’ belongs to the family Simaroubaceae. In traditional medicine, SG has been used as an anticancer, antiviral, antibacterial, and antihelminthic substance. Quassinoids (a category of degraded triterpene lactones) such as glaucarubolone, glaucarubin, and glaucarubinone are abundant in SG. Nutrapotent DS, a herbal formulation including S. glauca extract, has been found to possess anticancer properties and is used to treat cancer [31–33]. Simarouba glauca has a long history of use in herbal medicine and has a number of well-documented pharmacological characteristics [34]. There are reports on gold [35], silver [36,37], CuO [38], and nanoparticles from the S.glauca plant, but there are no reports on carbon dots from the same plant. Carbon dots has spent the last ten years focusing on cancer diagnosis and treatment [39]. Hence here, we are analyzing the anticancer potential of the synthesized carbon dot.

Herewith, a simple and effective technique for synthesizing CDs was developed using Simarouba glauca leaves as a carbon source for the first time. The synthesized CDs exhibited selective fluorescence and electrochemical sensing towards doxycycline (Scheme 1 ). We also demonstrate the application of these novel carbon dots as anticancer agents toward MCF-7 breast cancer cells. We believe that this work is distinctive in a variety of ways; a) the carbon dot is synthesized from Simarouba glauca leaves for the first time, b) fluorescence and electrochemical methods of analysis are also used for the detection of antibiotics.

The hydrothermal method was adopted for the synthesis of CDs. In a typical experiment, Simaroubaglauca leaf was mixed with distilled water and stirred for 15 min. The resultant solution was placed in a 250 mL Teflon-lined stainless-steel autoclave and heated at 170 °C for 10 h. Afterward, the autoclave was cooled to room temperature. The suspension was centrifuged for 10 min at 15,000 rpm to remove the bigger molecules, and further filtered using a 0.22 µm membrane to remove larger particles. The obtained pure solution was stored at 4 0C for further use.

A Shimadzu UV-3600 spectrometer (Japan) was used to record the UV–vis. absorption spectra. The Perkin Elmer-400 FT-IR spectrometer (Singapore) was used to conduct FT-IR experiments with a frequency range of 400–4000 cm−1. The fluorescent intensity was measured using the Shimadzu RF-6000 Spectro fluorophotometer. The structure was determined using a powder X-ray diffractometer (Bruker AXS D8 Advance X-ray diffractometer) using Cu Kα radiation at 1.5406 A0 wavelength. A high-resolution transmission electron microscope (HR-TEM) by a JEOL JEM-2100 microscope was used to examine the interior texture properties. The Horiba SZ-100 scientific nanoparticle analyzer (Japan) was used to perform particle size analysis and zeta potential studies. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) studies were conducted on a Thermo ScientificTM ESCALABTM Xi+ X-ray Photoelectron Spectrometer.

A three-electrode system in the Biologic SP 200 workstation was used for all electrochemical experiments. The working electrode was a CD-modified glassy carbon electrode (GCE), with the reference electrode being an Ag/AgCl electrode and the counter electrode being a Pt wire. Alumina slurry of 0.05 mM size was used to polish the bare GCE. The sample was drop cast on the electrode surface and dried at room temperature.

Sensitivity towards different analytes was evaluated by monitoring the PL intensity of the analytes as a function of wavelength at room temperature. In a typical procedure, a known volume and concentration of carbon dot and analytes were mixed in a cuvette, sonicated for 4 min, and a fluorescent spectrum was recorded. The sensitivity of the developed sensor towards Dox was tested by varying the concentration of Dox, ranging from 0 to 60 μM.

The bare GCE was polished with alumina slurry (0.05 M), and carbon dot was drop cast on the electrode surface. The selectivity of the sensor was analyzed by cyclic voltammetric experiments. The three-electrode approach was used to perform differential pulse voltammetry at various Dox concentrations (10–60 nM) in pH 6 buffer solutions.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of antibiotics in real samples

ce:section.ce:para: Doxycycline tablets were purchased at a local drug store. The antibiotic tablet was weighed correctly and ground to powder in order to prepare the sample. After putting a particular amount of pharmaceutical powder into a 10 mL centrifuge tube and adding HCl (10 mL, 1 M), the powder was dissolved under ultrasonic conditions [40]. Finally, the solution was tested with CDs.

The practical application of the CD as sensor was tested by using it to determine Dox levels in different water samples. Different water samples were collected, filtered using 0.22 millipore filter paper to remove undesired solid suspensions. These water samples were spiked with an adequate amount of Dox solutions and analyzed.

3 mL of PBS were reconstituted with 15 mg of MTT (Sigma, M-5655) before being filter sterilized. After the initial 24 h of incubation, the sample in each well was removed, and 30 l of reconstituted MTT solution was added to each test and cell control well. The plate was then gently shaken well, and the next 4 h were spent incubating at 37 0C in an incubator with humidified 5 % CO2The supernatant was taken out after the incubation period, 100 l of MTT Solubilization Solution (DMSO, Sigma Aldrich, USA) was added, and the wells were gently agitated by pipetting up and down in order to solubilize the formazan crystals. At a wavelength of 540 nm, the absorbance values were calculated using a microplate reader [41].

The percentage of growth inhibition was calculated using the formula:
Cell
v i a b i l i t y
%
=
Mean
O D
s a m p l e
x
100
Mean
O D
o f
c o n t r o l
g r o u p

The synthesized carbon dots were characterized by various techniques. The morphology of the carbon dots was analyzed by transmission electron microscopy (TEM). TEM images revealed that the CDs has a homogeneous spherical shape. The HRTEM picture showed that the CDs had an apparent lattice spacing of 0.295 nm (Fig. 1 a), which is consistent with the (00 2) graphite carbon plane [42]. The broad circular ring in the SAED micrograph pattern indicates the amorphous nature of the prepared CDs (Fig. 1b), and the size distribution histogram (Fig. 1c) reveals that CDs have an average size of 2.64 nm, ranging from 1 nm to 5 nm. Simultaneously, further confirmation with DLS experiments showed that the size varies between 1 and 10 nm, with an average size of 2.7 nm (Fig. S1). XRD peak observed at 2θ value 24.60 corresponds to diffraction from (002) lattice plane of amorphous carbon (Fig. 1d) [43,44].

The functional groups were identified using FT-IR spectroscopy. The FT-IR spectrum of CD shows a broad peak around 3400 cm−1, corresponding to OH/NH stretching vibrations (Fig. 1a). The characteristic peak at 2935, 1580, 1385, and 1055 cm−1 confirmed the presence of CH, CC, NH, and COO– respectively [45]. Raman analysis was carried out to determine the structural defects in CDs. The Raman image of exhibited two characteristic peaks at 1344 and 1575 cm−1, which corresponded to the disorder (D band) and graphite (G band) nanostructure of the CDs, respectively (Fig. 2 b). The D band is generated by the vibrations of carbon atoms with dangling bonds on the termination plane of the disordered graphite structure. The E2g vibration mode of graphite, associated with sp2 carbon atoms, is where the G band originates [46,47]. The synthesized CDs have lesser defective sites. The ID/IG ratio is linked to the degree of graphitization. CDs had an ID/IG of 0.76, indicating that they were graphitic.

In addition, XPS was used to investigate the surface elemental composition of CDs in more depth. Five peaks were visible in the CDs, corresponding to O1s, N1s, and C1s, respectively, at 533, 400, and 284 eV (Fig. 3 a) in the atomic ratio 23.83/3.5/72.67. The high-resolution C1s spectrum displayed three peaks, i.e., CC at 284 eV, CN/CO at 285.1 eV, and CO at 287 eV (Fig. 3b). The high-resolution O 1 s spectra showed three peaks at 530, 531.3, and 532.67 eV, corresponding to CO, CO, and C-OH/COC, respectively (Fig. 3c), and the high-resolution spectra of N1s exhibited peaks at 397.7, 398.9, and 401.38 eV, corresponds to CN, CN, and NH bonds (Fig. 3d) [48,49]. By combining the aforementioned findings with the FT-IR findings, we were able to confirm that the surface of CDs was rich in the OH and NH2 groups. EDX analysis further confirmed the abundance of carbon and oxygen elements in CD (Fig. S2).

Optical properties of as-synthesized CDs were evaluated by UV–vis. and fluorescence spectroscopy techniques. The UV–vis. absorption spectrum displayed a peak at 278.0 nm, which may be assigned to π -π* transition of CC group (Fig. 4 a). These CDs are brown under daylight and exhibit blue fluorescence under UV irradiation. Furthermore, CDs showed an excitation wavelength-dependent characteristic in the 300–370 nm range. With increasing excitation wavelength, the fluorescence emission peaks were gradually red-shifted from 380. Maximum emission intensity was obtained as 445 nm at an excitation wavelength of 365 nm (Fig. 4b).

The optical stability of the carbon dot was investigated in relation to ionic intensity, pH, and radiation time. Different ionic strength environments were simulated with varying concentrations (0–2 M) of KCl and NaCl. The fluorescence intensity showed no apparent variation in 0 to 2 M KCl (Fig. 5 a) and NaCl solutions (Fig. 5b). This establishes the excellent ionic stability of prepared CDs, which would be favorable for biosensing applications. Under 60 min of light irradiation, the fluorescence intensity of CDs remained nearly constant (Fig. 5c). The intensity of CD varied with pH and attained a maximum at pH 4. The stability of CDs is further confirmed by the negative zeta potential value (Fig. S3). The results show that CDs have excellent fluorescence stability. CDs as fluorescence sensors offer a lot of potentials in complicated systems, as demonstrated by the above qualities.

To obtain the optimum performance of the sensor, optimization studies were carried out. The effect of pH (3–10) and incubation time on the sensing performance were analyzed. Maximum quenching of Dox occurred at pH 6 (Fig. S4a), and is the optimum pH for the entire sensing platform. To ensure a full reaction, the reaction time was optimized. Following a 6 min incubation period, there were no discernible changes in the F/F0 signals (Fig. S4b). As a result, pH 6 and a 6 min interaction time were used in all subsequent sensing experiments.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of antibiotics by the CDs
ce:section.ce:para:
To establish the selectivity of the CDs to Dox, the effect of other kinds of antibiotics (5-Flurouracil (5-FU)), Amoxicillin, Ciprofloxacin (Cipro), Cloxacillin (Cloxa)), amino acids (Histidine, Cysteine), and biologically related molecules (Glucose, Mandelic acid (MA)), hazardous materials (Pyridine, 2,4-D, Acephate) were studied. Only doxycycline could clearly quench the fluorescence of the CDs, as shown in Fig. 6 a, and other compounds had minimal effect. The findings show that these CDs have high specificity for determining doxycycline antibiotics and are predicted to be an effective fluorescence sensor for Dox. As shown in Fig. 6b, the concentration of Dox (0–60 µM) was increased, and the PL intensity of CDs steadily decreased. The plot of F0/F against Dox concentration is shown in Fig. 6c. The quenching effectiveness of the CDs showed a good linear relationship in the linear range 2 to 20 µM with a linear correlation coefficient (R2) of 0.9959. The detection limit (LOD) was calculated as 0.79 µM using the equation.
(1)
LOD
=
3 × σ / S
where σ and S are the standard deviation of the signals and slope of the linear regression curve [18]. These findings indicate that the proposed sensor can be utilized as a fluorescence sensor of doxycycline. According to the bar graphs in Fig. 6d, Dox ions were able to efficiently quench CD emission intensity even while other analytes were interfering. These results showed how the suggested system could function as a special fluorescence probe to detect Dox ions in real water samples.

A variety of studies were carried out to investigate the sensing mechanism of Dox. The absorption spectrum of Dox and the excitation spectra of the CDs have a good spectral overlap (Fig. 7 a). This suggests that the quenching could be caused by the inner filter effect (IFE) or the fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET). To distinguish between IFE and FRET, fluorescence lifetime measurements were done. In the case of FRET, the fluorescence lifetime of the carbon dot-quencher system will drop or increase, whereas, in the case of IFE, it will remain the same or nearly equal [50,51]. The lifetime of CD and CD/Dox were measured as 4.31 ns and 4.26 ns (Fig. 7b). There are no apparent changes in the lifetime upon the addition of doxycycline. This indicates there is no energy transfer between CDs and Dox, and the possible quenching mechanism is an inner filter effect based static quenching mechanism. As a result, FRET was ruled out as a plausible mechanism for Dox quenching. The absorption spectra of CDs, Dox, and CDs with Dox were also studied (Fig. 7c), and no differences in the CD absorption spectra were seen when Dox was added. This proved the absence of ground-state complex formation. Thus, IFE acted as the predominant quenching mechanism.

Electrochemical sensing study was conducted using cyclic voltammetric measurement in the potential range of 0 to 1 V in phosphate buffer solution (PBS) with a scan rate of 50 mV/s to determine the detection efficiency of the synthesized sensor. In comparison to the bare glassy carbon electrode (GCE), the developed ternary sensor produced a threefold greater anodic peak current (Fig. 8 a). This demonstrates the absolute accuracy of detecting small levels of doxycycline.

pH optimization (pH 3–9) was carried out to find the optimum pH for Dox sensing. The maximum peak current was obtained at a pH of 6 (Fig. 8b). Thus pH 6 was selected for further studies. A scan rate investigation from 10 to 100 mV/s was carried out to get a better understanding of the redox process. The anodic peak current increased in proportion to the scan rate. The position of anodic peak current and peak potential show no difference, indicating that the process is reversible (Fig. 8c). The linear curve obtained by plotting the square root of the scan rate against peak current implies that the electrocatalytic process is diffusion-controlled (Fig. 8d). The succeeding studies were therefore conducted at a moderate scan rate of 50 mV/s.

The effect of the concentration of CD (2–10 mM) on the electrochemical performance of the sensor was evaluated. With an increase in the concentration of CDs, the current response was initially increased, reached a maximum at a concentration of 4 mM, and decreased with a further increase in concentration (Fig. S5). So, entire experiments were conducted at pH 6, with a scan rate of 50 mV/s, and 4 mM concentration of CD.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Reproducibility, stability analysis, PEIS, and photocurrent density
ce:section.ce:para: The stability of the sensor was determined by comparing the CV runs of a fresh sample with one that had been held in a desiccator for one month. After one month, the sensor had a well-defined peak current with just a minor drop in peak current intensity, indicating remarkable stability and reproducibility (Fig. 10 a). The reusability and repeatability of the modified electrodes are the most crucial problems with regard to the use of electrochemical sensors. Cyclic voltammetric analysis for a cycle number of 60 was used to test the repeatability and reusability of the sensor. When comparing the first and last analysis cycles, the peak current shows only minor variance, indicating that the developed sensor has great repeatability and reusability (Fig. 10b). The reproducibility of 4 different modified GCE was estimated by CV analysis. The result was determined to be satisfactory, as revealed by the relative standard deviation (RSD) of 1.25 % for peak currents from four subsequent measurements, as shown in Fig. S6.

Potentio-electrochemical impedance spectroscopy was used to test the activity of the electrochemical sensor (PEIS). The impedance spectra measured at frequencies ranging from 5 Hz to 150 MHz in 5 mM [Fe(CN)6]3-/4- and 0.1 M KCl solutions are displayed in Fig. 10c. The spectrum depicts a semicircle that represents its resistance. The produced sensor exhibits decreased resistance, indicating an increased electron transfer reaction rate. The electroanalytical capabilities of the developed senor towards the sensing of Dox was evaluated by measuring photocurrent density. The intensity of photocurrent increases with the addition of doxycycline (Fig. 10d).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Possible mechanism for the electrochemical sensing of doxycycline
ce:section.ce:para: When no doxycycline is present, there is no oxidation peak in the scan range. Still, when 1 mM doxycycline is present, there is a minor oxidation peak at 0.6 V. This suggests oxidation of doxycycline on the surface of the modified electrode. The distinct ionizable functionalities in doxycycline undergo changes with pH. At our optimized pH (pH 6), protonation occurs. Hence these positively charged molecules interact with negatively charged functionalities like COO– and OH– in CDs surface leads to electrochemical sensing [56].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of doxycycline in real samples
ce:section.ce:para: The recoveries of Dox in antibiotic tablets were estimated to be 100.59–103.2 %, with RSDs ranging from 2.1 to 4.8 %. Furthermore, when three different concentrations of Dox were added to water samples (5, 10, and 15 µM), average recoveries were achieved, for example, 99.95–100.12 % for river water and 99–99.73 % for lake water with RSD less than 5 % (Fig. 11 ). The findings suggest that the sensor could detect doxycycline ions in the spiked samples with high precision, indicating that it has improved practical applications.

In conclusion, a novel fluorescent probe based on CDs from Simarouba glauca leaves was developed for the detection of doxycycline. The synthesized CDs exhibited blue fluorescence and excellent stability. Due to the innerl filter effect (IFE), doxycycline was capable of quenching the fluorescence of CDs, laying the groundwork for quantifying doxycycline. The CDs, as a dual-sensor, could detect doxycycline in fluorescence and electrochemical methods with good detection limits. Moreover, CDs not only were used for the detection of doxycycline in antibiotic tablets and water samples, but also could act as anticancer agents against MCF-7 human breast cancer cells. The dual sensor based on CDs has a fantastic prospect for advanced analytical applications.

Sneha Mathew: Conceptualization, Methodology, Resources, Writing – original draft. Chinnu R. Thara: Visualization, Data curation. Neenamol John: Visualization, Data curation. Beena Mathew: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Project administration, Conceptualization.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: Greatly acknowledges the financial assistance received from the University Grants Commission (UGC) in the form of Junior Research Fellowship (JRF).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.jphotochem.2022.114237.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary data 1

sb:title.sb:maintitle: A dual-emission nano-rod MOF equipped with carbon dots for visual detection of doxycycline and sensitive sensing of MnO4 −
RSC Adv.

Microchim. Acta

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Recent advances in sensors for tetracycline antibiotics and their applications
TrAC, Trends Anal. Chem.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: X. Liu, D. Huang, C. Lai, G. Zeng, L. Qin, C. Zhang, Recent advances in sensors for tetracycline antibiotics and their applications. TrAC Trends in Analytical Chemistry 109 (2018) 260-274.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Determination of doxycycline in chicken fat by liquid chromatography with UV detection and liquid chromatography–tandem mass spectrometry
J. Chromatogr. B
